# It’s time to leave legacy SIEM behind

The future belongs to the open security lakehouse

Ali Ghodsi & Ben Horowitz's RSA 2026 Keynote Replay

The rules of physics have changed. Your security must too.

We are in the midst of the largest secular shift of our lifetimes. Cyberattacks have transitioned from manual operations to autonomous AI swarms that weaponize vulnerabilities in hours, not months. Traditional security architectures, built for human-scale, have reached an architectural dead end.

In this 45-minute RSA keynote, Ali Ghodsi (Databricks Co-founder and CEO) and Ben Horowitz (Andreessen Horowitz Co-founder and General Partner) reveal why the "Petabyte Era" requires a fundamental shift from human-centric to machine-centric defense.

Key Takeaways:

- Zero-Day Weaponization: Understand the new reality where the time-to-exploit has shrunk from years to just 1.3 days, making manual SOC processes obsolete.
- The End of the "Data Tax": Stop paying to throw away data. Learn how legacy SIEM pricing forces you to discard vital telemetry, creating unsurvivable gaps in the age of AI.
- Multimodal Defense: Learn why traditional security tools are blind to 2026’s biggest threats and how to leverage multimodal data like video, audio, and chat data to stop threats like social engineering.
- Fight Agents with Agents: Discover how Lakewatch uses swarms of AI agents to automate detection, triage, and investigation natively where your data lives.
- The Power of Openness: See how an open ecosystem—built on Unity Catalog and OCSF—replaces proprietary black boxes with a unified open security lakehouse.

# Ali Ghodsi, Co-Founder and CEO of Databricks

Jul 16, 2021

Share

In this episode of Talks at GS, Ali Ghodsi, co-founder and CEO of Databricks, discusses how his personal journey has influenced his entrepreneurial and leadership style, his company’s mission to build the future of data and AI for all enterprises, and key insights to how Databricks drives innovation within the tech landscape.

On the state of AI: “It’s very early…I know this is cliché, but Marc Andreessen said, ‘software is eating the world.’ He meant software is in your Apple Watch, it’s in your thermostat, it’s in your Tesla car, everywhere it’s software. I really think AI will follow software. Wherever you have software, you’re going to collect data and you’re going to automate things. It’s going to be more intelligence. So, you get more intelligent software…we’re still in the ‘software is eating the world’ kind of phase. So… it’s very early.

On entrepreneurial and leadership style: “Try to simulate…how they feel, or what they want, or what they want to buy, or how they feel about your products, or how they feel about how you’re treating them as employees…If you can empathize and really simulate how people are thinking, you can much better connect to them. And I think that’s really, really crucial.”

The episode was recorded on June 17, 2021.

# Data Alone Is Not Enough: The Evolution of Data Architectures

Ali Ghodsi and Martin Casado Posted October 22, 2020

#### Data Alone Is Not Enough: The Evolution of Data Architectures Table of Contents

Share

Share

Email

X

Facebook

Flipboard

Reddit

Data, data, data — it’s long been a buzzword in the industry, whether big data, streaming data, data analytics, data science, even AI & machine learning — but data alone is not enough: it takes an entire system of tools and technology to extract value from data.

A multibillion dollar industry has emerged around data tools and technologies. And with so much excitement and innovation in the space: how exactly do all these tools fit together?

This podcast — a hallway style conversation between Ali Ghodsi, CEO and founder of Databricks, and a16z general partner Martin Casado — explores the evolution of data architectures, including some quick history, where they’re going, and a surprising use case for streaming data, as well as Ali’s take on how he’d architect the picks and shovels that handle data end-to-end today.

## Transcript

### A brief history of data architectures

Ali: It kind of started in the ’80s. Business leaders were flying blind, not knowing how the business was doing, waiting for finance to close the books. This data warehousing paradigm came about where they said, “Look, we have all this data in these operational data systems. Why don’t we just get all that data, take it out of all these systems, transform it into a central place, let’s call it a data warehouse, and then we can get business intelligence on that data?”

## Spark Summit Keynotes

Although the October overcast persisted over Brussels, inside the SQUARE’s convention center attendees lined up, with coffee in one hand and pastry in the other, to hear how other organizations employ Apache Spark for their use cases.

## Democratizing AI with Apache Spark

The second day kicked off with the a keynote from Databricks CEO Ali Ghodsi on the topic of artificial intelligence (AI).

Ali observed that the machine learning algorithm itself is rarely the main barrier in building AI applications. Instead, the real culprit is the set of complex systems that manages the infrastructure and prepares the data for the ML algorithms.

According to Ali, Spark is a huge leap forward in democratizing AI because of its speed, flexibility, and scalability. However, Spark cannot solve all the problems around AI by itself - and this is where Databricks comes in. Databricks’ vision is to build a platform around Spark that allows organizations to easily capitalize on Spark’s inherent speed, flexibility, and scalability for advanced analytics and beyond.

As another step towards Databricks’ goal, Ali announced the addition of GPU support and integration of popular deep learning libraries to the Databricks’ big data platform. This allows organizations to easily conduct deep learning on Spark using the popular TensorFlow framework on top of highly optimized GPU hardware.

The deep learning functionality works in concert with other components of the Databricks platform, enabling organizations to seamlessly perform data wrangling, feature extraction, interactive exploration, and model training in an end-to-end machine learning pipeline. Read the blog on GPU support to learn more, or contact us to get started.

## Description

The Rise of the Lakehouse:
Data warehouses have a long history in decision support and business intelligence applications. But, data warehouses were not well suited to dealing with the unstructured, semi-structured, and streaming data common in modern enterprises. This led to organizations building data lakes of raw data about a decade ago. But, they also lacked important capabilities. The need for a better solution has given rise to lakehouse architecture, which implements similar data structures and data management features to those in a data warehouse, directly on the kind of low cost storage used for data lakes.

This keynote by Databricks CEO, Ali Ghodsi, explains how the open source Delta Lake project allows the industry to realize the full potential of lakehouse architecture. Additionally, Ali will discuss the newly announced SQL Analytics service that allows users to run traditional analytics on their data lake, instead of moving data out to data warehouses, without sacrificing performance, security, or quality. This service completes the vision of lakehouse architecture to allow the data lake to be a single source of truth of all data workloads.

Ali Ghodsi is the CEO and co-founder of Databricks, a leader in data and AI. He pioneered the data intelligence platform built on a lakehouse architecture, revolutionizing analytics and AI for global organizations. Prior to becoming CEO, he served as the VP of engineering and product management. In this conversation with Stanford adjunct lecturer Ravi Belani, Ghodsi Shares lessons he’s learned from being one of seven co-founders at Databricks and leading a company that began with open-source software.

# TED, Khan Academy, ETS launch AI-focused higher education institute | ETIH EdTech News — EdTech Innovation Hub

Published: 2026-04-16T01:00:17+01:00
Author: Written By Emma Thompson

## Summary

TED, Khan Academy, and ETS have announced plans to launch the Khan TED Institute, a higher education initiative built around artificial intelligence, skills measurement, and employer-defined competencies. The institute will use a model based on competency progression, applied AI learning, and direct links to workforce requirements. Students will be assessed based on demonstrated competency rather than time spent in education, allowing learners to progress at their own pace while completing defined skill benchmarks. Corporate partners including Google, Microsoft, Accenture, Bain & Company, McKinsey & Company and Replit will contribute to curriculum design and the definition of competency benchmarks, linking learning outcomes to employer expectations.

# Biohub Launches the Virtual Biology Initiative to Galvanize a Global Effort to Create the Open Data Foundation for AI-Accelerated Biology

Published: 2026-04-29T05:00:00-04:00
Author: Biohub
Type: Press Release

## Summary

Biohub has launched the Virtual Biology Initiative, a five-year initiative to create the Open Data Foundation for AI-Accelerated Biology. The initiative aims to use $500 million in funding to develop predictive models of the human cell to accelerate the cure and prevention of all disease. Biohub is committing $100 million to support this effort, $400 million to generate data at scale and develop next-generation technologies for measuring, imaging, and engineering biology. The data generated by Biohub will be made open and freely available as a resource for the worldwide scientific community. Other institutions involved include the Allen Institute, Arc Institute, Broad Institute, and Wellcome Sanger Institute. NVIDIA will support the initiative to leverage accelerated computing infrastructure, domain-specific software, and technical expertise to generate large-scale datasets.

## Ali Ghodsi

#### AI & Data Analytics Visionary; Co-Founder & CEO of Databricks; Lakehouse Architecture Creator

Ali Ghodsi is the co-founder and CEO of Databricks, a platform specializing in data analytics and artificial intelligence that has influenced how organizations manage and analyze large datasets. Ghodsi has overseen the development of the lakehouse architecture, which integrates data lakes and warehouses to support scalable AI and machine learning workflows. This approach has been widely adopted by enterprises seeking efficient big data processing solutions.

Ghodsi's background includes significant contributions to open-source projects such as Apache Spark, which forms the foundation of Databricks' technology and is widely used in data engineering. He has led the company through periods of growth, introducing innovations like Delta Lake for reliable data storage and Unity Catalog for data governance, reinforcing Databricks' position in the AI and cloud computing sectors.

Ghodsi is recognized for connecting academic research with practical enterprise applications, making complex topics in distributed systems and cloud computing accessible to a broad audience.

Ghodsi frequently appears at technology conferences, where he discusses topics such as AI scalability and data intelligence. He continues to guide Databricks' advancements in generative AI tools. Ghodsi has been included in influential rankings among AI leaders, reflecting his expertise in demystifying emerging technologies. His public talks often address challenges in data infrastructure, encouraging audiences to leverage the full potential of data analytics and artificial intelligence.

# Databricks CEO and Co-Founder Ali Ghodsi Announced as a Keynote Speaker for Money20/20 USA
NEW YORK, Sept. 25, 2023 —[Money20/20](https://us.money2020.com/)is thrilled to announce Ali Ghodsi, the esteemed Co-Founder and CEO of[Databricks](https://databricks.com), as a keynote speaker at this year’s show in Las Vegas.
[![](https://www.bigdatawire.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/10/Ali-Ghodsi-1-300x300.png)](https://www.bigdatawire.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/10/Ali-Ghodsi-1.png) 
*Databricks CEO and co-founder Ali Ghodsi*
Databricks recently announced they secured more than $500 million USD in a funding round that values the company at $43 billion USD, marking one of the biggest funding events for private tech companies this year. New investors include NVIDIA and financial firm Capital One Ventures.
For over a decade, Databricks has been bringing data, analytics and AI to companies around the world. With the recent acquisition of MosaicML, the combined companies plan to make generative AI accessible to any size organization, enabling them to build, own and secure generative AI models with their data. Ali Ghodsi will take the stage to deliver a captivating fireside talk on the transformative impact of generative AI and how it is reshaping the landscape of the financial services industry.

Ali Ghodsi is the CEO and Co–founder of Databricks, responsible for the growth and international​ ​expansion of the company. In addition to his work at Databricks, Ali serves as an adjunct professor at UC Berkeley and is on the board at UC Berkeley’s RiseLab. Ali was one of the creators of open source project Apache Spark, and ideas from his academic research have been applied to Apache Mesos​ ​and Apache Hadoop.​ ​​Ali​ ​received his MBA from Mid-Sweden University in 2003 and PhD from KTH/Royal Institute of Technology in Sweden​ in 2006​ in the area of​ ​Distributed Computing.

## Ali Ghodsi

#### AI & Data Analytics Visionary; Co-Founder & CEO of Databricks; Lakehouse Architecture Creator

Ali Ghodsi is the co-founder and CEO of Databricks, a platform specializing in data analytics and artificial intelligence that has influenced how organizations manage and analyze large datasets. Ghodsi has overseen the development of the lakehouse architecture, which integrates data lakes and warehouses to support scalable AI and machine learning workflows. This approach has been widely adopted by enterprises seeking efficient big data processing solutions.

Ghodsi's background includes significant contributions to open-source projects such as Apache Spark, which forms the foundation of Databricks' technology and is widely used in data engineering. He has led the company through periods of growth, introducing innovations like Delta Lake for reliable data storage and Unity Catalog for data governance, reinforcing Databricks' position in the AI and cloud computing sectors.

Ghodsi is recognized for connecting academic research with practical enterprise applications, making complex topics in distributed systems and cloud computing accessible to a broad audience.

Ghodsi frequently appears at technology conferences, where he discusses topics such as AI scalability and data intelligence. He continues to guide Databricks' advancements in generative AI tools. Ghodsi has been included in influential rankings among AI leaders, reflecting his expertise in demystifying emerging technologies. His public talks often address challenges in data infrastructure, encouraging audiences to leverage the full potential of data analytics and artificial intelligence.

Nutanix, TransUnion and Q2 also big gainers.
>> Meantime, startup Databricks announcing some key milestones today, including a $4 billion revenue run rate in Q2. That's up 50% year over year. Its AI products crossing above $1 billion in revenue run rate as well. Plus the company closing a fresh round of funding $1 billion worth of funding at a valuation of more than $100 billion. But some investors are beginning to question some of the AI adoption thesis.
Apollo's Torsten Slok, pointing out that AI adoption among large firms is actually declining. That's according to US Census Bureau data. Joining us now in a first on CNBC interview, is Databricks co-founder and CEO Ali Gadzhi.
Ali, it's great to have you on the show. Welcome.
>> Thank you. Glad to be here.
>> So let's start right there with the fact that you've got a $4 billion run rate for revenue in Q2. It's up 50% year over year. What's driving it. Can it continue.
>> Yeah. So we're seeing lots and lots of momentum behind two of our products. The first one is called Agent Bricks. And that product really helps you build agents that are helping out in the workplace to automate sort of work that you have to do everyday tasks. And what's different here is a lot of the AI labs have been really focused on super intelligence, you know, math olympiads and programing contests. We're more focused on just everyday tasks that you need to do. And we're seeing huge uptick in the adoption of this. So it's actually accelerating our revenue. And the second one is a database that we built called Lake Base which supports these agents because these agents all need a database to store things in. And they use these databases differently from humans. So yeah, we're actually seeing I've actually a long time been saying that, you know, there's a bubble that AI is maybe, you know, overheated.
But actually we're finally seeing the green shoots and some of these use cases actually taking off.
>> So we just talked about the research by Torsten Slok, looking at the decline among major companies in terms of that investment into AI.
Doesn't sound like you're seeing it. We also have been talking quite a bit about cash burn. Most recently, the disclosures from OpenAI and how much they're spending. You achieved positive free cash flow. Can that continue? And I guess to go back to this bubble question, do you think that we're starting to see this formulation of winners and losers and normalization as AI becomes more prevalent?
>> Yeah, I mean, so we were very careful with this. You know, should we also invest, should we get insane number of GPUs and so on. And that's when we decided, no, we're going to run this like a public company but in private markets. So that's why 12 last months we were cash flow positive and for a long time have been saying we should be careful. Let's not get overexcited about AI. And, you know, I think there was an over promise and people overinvested and now it's kind of coming back down. But actually the thing that's so interesting is that just now the actual use cases are starting to work and they're actually starting to get deployed. So interesting to see what happens with these expectations. You know, can we catch up with these production use cases of AI that are just starting to catch up, or is it just there's too much excitement and people overspent and there's going to be a correction.
>> On that. Ali, first of all, good to see you. It's been a while. It seems before customers can get the benefit of AI Agentic AI, they have to have their data organized, tagged, managed. I'm hearing that is especially a challenge for larger and older enterprises that have lots of data silos from acquisitions, lots of unstructured data. How much of a bottleneck is that to AI adoption and monetization?
How do you clear it?
>> Yeah, that's actually a great point, John. It's actually the governance of the data that's the problem. It's not like we can't access that data. That data is there. So why are we just not using it then for AI? It's because of privacy. Because at the other side of this, corporations are super worried about their most sensitive data. They want to be really careful who gets access to it, you know, is it going to get hacked? Are we going to have a leak? So that's also hampering access. So then how do you govern that data in a secure, safe way, in a privacy preserving way? This is why we focus a lot on that. At Databricks, we built a whole solution called Unity Catalog that just does that. And it's actually the number one sort of prerequisite before you can use AI. So that's the other. That's the other side of this. That's what holding back the AI because people are nervous about their data.
>> So let me get to this lake base product as well. Help me understand this conundrum that I see potentially with large customer adoption. There's some of your highest potential customers, but they're also likely to have databases they've been working with for a long time, like like Oracle, which reports tomorrow, maybe MongoDB for some users. Can you give an example with Lake Base of how the adoption cycle with large customers plays out, how you expect to gain share?
>> Yeah, that's an excellent question. For the last 40 years, this database market hasn't changed that much. The thing that has actually changed is in the last three years, we're now seeing it in our data. We looked at we tracked who's creating these databases inside Databricks. And for the first time, over 80% of the databases are being created by AI agents.
So for the first time in the history of mankind, it's no longer humans. They used to be called database administrators, DBAs. They're not the ones creating the databases. It's a new customer. It's the agent.
So we're really focused on helping them. And we think that's the way you can actually disrupt that whole database market. What's really happening in the market, John, is that previously enterprises were buying SaaS applications, and these SaaS applications could do all kinds of things with a system of record data that they had. But more and more, what's happening now is that enterprises are saying, you know, we'll just vibe code using AI, our own software, ourselves, and that vibe coded software needs a database behind it, and it needs to be really good for the vibe coded agents that they're building.
That's where Lake Based comes in. So I think you're going to see this trend more and more, not just about our lake base, but that in the industry as a whole, enterprises are building their own software software in-house, using vibe coding, because the costs of building software has just come down dramatically now, thanks to AI.
>> Ali, before we let you go, you said you've been running the company like a public company. Any plans to IPO?
>> Well, you know, as I said, you know, if we were planning on long term being private, we would have been burning 10 billion a year or something. So we're not doing that. So the fact that we're cash flow breakeven means that, you know, we want to run it like a public company. We have public company financial audits that happen, but we're not in a hurry. # Databricks Data+AI Summit 2025 Keynote | JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon
- Channel: Techusiness
- Length: 40:11
- Views: 1.6K
- Language: en
## Description
On June 11, 2025, during the Data+AI Summit 2025 Keynote in San Francisco, Jamie Dimon, Chairman and CEO of JPMorgan Chase, and Ali Ghodsi, CEO of Databricks, engaged in a conversation about the transformative role of data and AI in the financial sector. Dimon highlighted the immense challenge of managing data, noting that JPMorgan Chase processes $10 trillion daily, stating, “The hardest part is the data. It dwarfs everything.” The discussion explored AI strategies, the need for workforce upskilling, and enterprise priorities, aligning with the summit’s theme, “Data Intelligence for All.”
Dimon stressed that managing vast data volumes, such as JPMorgan’s $10 trillion daily transactions, is a bigger hurdle than AI technology itself. This underscores the need for robust data infrastructure to support AI initiatives.
The talk covered JPMorgan’s approach to integrating AI, including modernizing data preparation with Databricks’ platform, and the need to upskill employees to use AI effectively.
Dimon discussed breaking down internal barriers to fast-track AI adoption while maintaining compliance, offering insights into strategic priorities for financial institutions.

# $46B of hard truths: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear | Ben Horowitz (a16z)
- Channel: Lenny's Podcast

## Description
Ben Horowitz is the co-founder of Andreessen Horowitz, Silicon Valley’s largest and most influential venture capital firm, with over $46B in committed capital across multiple funds. He took Loudcloud public with just $2 million in revenue (dubbed “the IPO from hell”), sold it for $1.6 billion, and has backed companies from Facebook to Stripe to Airbnb to OpenAI to Databricks (now worth more than $100 billion). His management philosophy—forged through near-death experiences and refined through coaching hundreds of CEOs—contradicts most conventional startup wisdom.
*In our conversation, Ben shares:*
1. Why “founder mode” is half right and half dangerously wrong
2. The story behind “Good Product Manager/Bad Product Manager” and why it went viral despite being written in anger
3. Where the biggest AI startup opportunities remain
4. Why you need to run toward fear, never away
5. The one trait that predicts that a founder will fail as CEO
6. Inside Paid in Full, Ben’s nonprofit awarding pensions to pioneering hip-hop artists

# The Ultimate PySpark Cheat Sheet for Production & Interviews | by Ali Hassan | Apr, 2026 | Medium
Published: 2026-04-11T09:43:42.140000+00:00
Author: Ali Hassan
## Summary
Spark does not immediately execute transformations, but builds a logical execution plan (DAG), which allows Spark to optimize the entire query. DAG stands for Directed Acyclic Graph and helps Spark optimize the execution plan and provide fault tolerance. Narrow and Wide transformations are different operations that require data shuffling across the network. Data is only processed when an Action (like or ) is called. Data Skew can cause one node to perform 90% of the work while others sit idle. The default storage level (MEMORYAND_DISK) can be specified. The DataFrame is high-level, schema-based (like tables), highly optimized by Catalyst, and Dataset is strictly typed DataFrames. However, these errors can lead to significant performance overhead.

# Tirthankar Lahiri, Oracle | Oracle Data Deep Dive 2026 - Channel: SiliconANGLE theCUBE - Length: 20:26 - Views: 1.6K - Likes: 428 - Published: 2026-04-15T11:20:01-07:00 - Category: People & Blogs - Language: en ## Description In this interview from Oracle Data Deep Dive NYC 2026, Tirthankar Lahiri, senior vice president of mission-critical data and AI engines at Oracle, joins theCUBE's Dave Vellante to discuss how embedding agentic AI capabilities directly into the database is helping enterprises move beyond pilots to production-scale AI deployments. Lahiri explains how the transition from RAG-based chatbots to multi-step agentic workflows is reshaping data architecture requirements. He outlines Oracle's strategy of co-locating agentic processing with data to eliminate fragmentation, reduce round trips and ensure agents act on current, consistent information. Central to this approach is the Unified Memory Core — allowing short-term, medium-term and long-term memory constructs to be derived directly from existing database data, removing the need for separate storage systems for each memory type. The conversation also explores how Oracle is tackling the security risks unique to agentic environments. Lahiri introduces "deep data security," a policy-based approach that enforces identity-aware access controls at the data layer rather than the application tier — making it resilient to prompt injection, malformed queries and unauthorized access regardless of how an agent enters the system. He details new offerings including the AI Database Private Agent Factory, the Private AI Services Container for fully air-gapped deployments and Trusted Answer Search, which uses vector search rather than LLMs to guarantee hallucination-free reporting. From the "Vectors on Ice" capability for vectorized access to Apache Iceberg tables to a commitment to making all of these capabilities available at no additional cost, Lahiri provides a roadmap for how organizations can make the database the operational hub of their AI strategy. Find more SiliconANGLE news and analysis https://siliconangle.com/ Follow theCUBE's wall-to-wall event coverage https://siliconangle.com/events/ Learn about the latest theCUBE events https://www.thecube.net/ #theCUBE #OracleDataDeepDiveNYC #theCUBEresearch #Oracle #AI #DataArchitecture ## Chapters - 00:00 Intro - 00:07 Exploring the Landscape of Agentic AI: From Foundations to Innovations - 02:46 Integrating Data Control and Agent Proximity - 05:35 Data-Driven Memory: The Core of Business Intelligence - 07:40 Empowering AI-Driven Solutions: User Empowerment, Customization, and Risk Management - 11:23 Securing Trust: Navigating Deep Data Security and Customer Concerns in NY - 14:52 Navigating the Future: Vector Search, LLMs, and Open Standards in Agent Specification - 17:54 Conclusion and Final Thoughts ## Transcript We're back here at the cub's NYC wired studios at the New York Stock Exchange. I'm Dave Volante and we're going to talk about Agentic AI innovations. This is Oracle Data Deep Dive NYC. Tthanka La is here. He's the senior vice president for mission critical data and AI engines at Oracle. >> Thank you for coming in, sir. Appreciate it. >> Thank you for having >> How's the week going for you guys over at Javitz? You guys got done some deep dives. A lot of the engineering teams were in today, right? >> Yep. It was amazing. Very, very uplifting. Great. >> Yeah. Awesome. >> Well, anyway, this year, you know, we're seeing this shift toward a gentic. We remember, we all remember the sort of ragbased chatbot days. Make a request, get an answer, and then sort of reasoning came in, >> which was a sort of a whole new era. Now we're into this multi-agent agentic era where people are customers are are executing multi-step uh uh agent actions and you're coordinating with other other agents. This puts a lot of pressure on you guys. You're embedding a lot of these capabilities into the data layer, >> right? >> I wonder if you could just at a high level just to kick us off, you know, what problems are you trying to solve that your customers are coming to you? What what kind of problems does this new era create? And then we'll get into kind of what it means for organizations. >> Yeah, great. That's a great question, a great kickoff question. So, um, Agentic systems are going to become the future application development. They're the present and they will be the future. >> They're the way people build enterprise applications. Many organizations are still struggling though to realize value from agents because ultimately agents are only as good as their data. So, this is why we have announced new agentic AI innovations by architecting agentic AI deep into the database. uh so that will enable customers to move beyond pilots and failed PC's to full-scale production workloads. Um the three main sort of themes we're following with this agentic journey is to allow customers to innovate faster with AI for data by uh building capabilities for agentic AI such as unified memory core deep into Oracle database. Um we also wanted uh want customers to be able to avoid AI data risk. So provide agentic processing that is safe and secure and trustworthy within the database. And lastly, this world is changing so fast. You can't lock yourself into a certain certain direction now. It's too early for that. So our our approach is to use open standards, open formats as much as possible to give the customer the full choice and flexibility of the entire landscape of offerings that exist today. And these capabilities are all very flexible. They're available on all environments, on premises, multicloud, hybrid cloud, and uh public cloud environments. >> All right, thank you for that setup. So, let's double click on that. When I was prepping for this interview, I was watching an interview that you did with John Furrier and he said he said essentially Oracle is effectively making the data layer the control plane or the control hub for aentic AI. I said, okay, that's interesting. And I said to myself, all right, what does that mean from an architectural standpoint? Because I there are organizations out there technology companies that are saying okay we're going to we're going to be the orchestrator of agents that's their layer there are others are saying well you know we want to be sort of the the knowledge graph you know etc. >> You guys are architecting the entire sort of stack from from hardware all the way up to the applications and obviously the data in between. So I wonder if you could elaborate on this idea of the data layer as the control plane and what that means architecturally. >> Right. That's a great question. So our approach is to try to bring agentic processing as close to the data as possible >> so that the agents can act on current consistent and real time data. There are basically two types of agents. There's reasoning centric agents and datacentric agents. And agents are going to be a hierarchy or a graph. The datacentric agents are really best run coll-located with data. That's what our approach is. Um, we want to eliminate the need for multiple round trips, multiple database accesses. It's a bit like you'd want to take a direct flight, not multiple stops on the way to get to destination. And um, architecting agentic processing along with data access avoids fragmented or fractured AI. you get AI that runs again on clean real time current data without the need to split your data in multiple repositories. So if I can follow up on that, I I I get that you want to have, you know, the proximity because of latency, right? >> Does it also reduce the number of hops that you have to take as you just described and why? >> Yes. Um there are other uh vendors who have advocated for a um diverse database strategy where you use a graph database for graph interactions and a document for document interactions. We think that's the wrong model because that ends up with first of all a giant sprawl of these data stacks. Um it ends up also with inconsistency across the different data views. So with Oracle database we support all of those data formats and representations on the same underlying unified data. So that way you can use whatever data model is appropriate for agentic processing without the need to move data back and forth between different systems. So you it's not just the latency benefits, it's also efficiency, it's real-time access and it's the fact that I can view that same data in multiple formats depending on the need of the agent. >> So when earlier we were talking to Juan Loa and we were we were talking about unified memory core and the way I understood it is it's memory. It's short-term memory, long-term memory. humans have have both and and and I wonder if we could get your technical perspective on this, you know, what is that? What's new and different about it? And and why does it matter for customers? >> It's a great question. You know, I love this because memory for agents is just like human memory. We have short-term memory. We have medium-term memory. We have long-term memory or facts. So agents work exactly the same way. And you need different types of representations to encode those memories properly. In databases, data begins life in tables and rows and columns, maybe documents, but they're not the facts the agent actually makes use of. Um, what we have built with Oracle is a unified core where on that existing database data, you can derive the memory constructs you need for each memory use case. Sometimes you want associations and you want a knowledge graph. Sometimes you just want a factual representation of an event that happened. Sometimes you want to know that so and so was the highly paid most highly paid employee in a department at a certain point in time. These are all derived ultimately from actual data. That derivation if it's done in place with the actual data is current, it's consistent and it's fully secure. We call that the unified memory core for that reason which is much more efficient than using multiple storage systems to represent the different kinds of memory. It's just like a person, the most effective person has everything in their head. They don't go to the library to the bookstore to to various places to find information. They they all have it. The best lawyer knows everything up front. Oracle database strives to be that unified memory core to enable the most efficient agentic processing. >> All right. Well, that makes sense. Um, and we we always say that it's not just the LLM. LM's obviously super important. It's not just the agent, but it's the data that's going to give you that competitive advantage. >> The other observation of course is that we're now speaking to the data. >> Yes. you know, even with ragbased chat bots, it was like, you know, how do you do this or what is that or, you know, explain this? Now, it's build me something. Um, so that's different. Now, as a business user, um, obviously developers, they got their hands full. They're doing doing a lot of the work. It they're doing their thing. But as a business unit, I'm going to want to interact with my data. How do I do that? Can I can I do that simply, safely, without having to necessarily go through the IT department uh or the developers that are maybe backlogged, right? >> Um maybe they got other priorities. Can I directly go to the data and interact with it, build things as a you know citizen builder if I will and how do you do that? How do you enable that? How do you make it safe? >> Right? We love it and developers of course we want to empower them. However, we want to make it easy for users to build agents as well. So one of the offerings in that space we're calling the AI database private agent factory that basically is exactly what it says it is. It's a factory for create creating agents simply where the end user has full control over the agentic workflow over why decisions are made in a certain way. They control that reasoning process. It's a very simple drag and drop based visual builder for agents that contains custom prompts. You can add your own prompt to it. Um it allows very quick and rapid agent assembly. We're also develop we're also we have just rolled out limited availability an autonomous AI vector database that can enable super fast uh very easy to access vector search against your business data which is again endemic to agentic processing you need to find similar data when you're doing agentic reasoning and vector search excels at that and autonomous vector database lets you consume that very easily so these two things combined the agent factory and vector datab uh AI vector database will enable that seamless harmony between agentic processing and data processing for developers and end users. >> Okay, thanks for that. I want to ask you about um mitigating risk. So, >> you know, when we're using chat bots, it hallucinates, which it does. Yeah. >> Gives you the wrong answer. >> Um you know, that kind of they're easy to manipulate with, oh no, you're wrong. This that and the other thing, okay, that's no big deal. Consumer applications, etc. But when I have agents actually taking action on my behalf, >> that becomes very risky. Yes, it can make a wrong move. It could leak certain data. Um, I mean that could not only violate compliance edicts. I mean, if it's egregious enough, I could actually go to jail or, you know, experience huge fines. >> So, there's the compliance, there's that data risk, there's security. All of this changes with with the Gentic and and in this AI era. How is Oracle dealing with that? >> That is a very very relevant question today. AI data security especially where we're sitting right now is super important because this information is business critical and yes you can go to jail for diverging the wrong information. Um the problem is LLMs are statistical machines. Um even as they are getting better but they will make mistakes and sometimes you know they could go horribly wrong and give you the wrong query. # Eightfold AI Lawsuit Exposes Why Enterprise AI Hiring Needs Deep Engineering | Medium
Published: 2026-04-27T05:47:19.117000+00:00
Author: Ashutosh Singhal
## Summary
The Eightfold AI lawsuit, Kistler v. Eightfold, has been brought to court alleging that the company secretly profiles individuals and uses their profiles to determine their economic futures. The lawsuit argues that Eightfold’s platform uses data from LinkedIn, GitHub, Crunchbase, and other public sources to create a “match score” from 0 to 5 that predicts your “likelihood of success.” Companies like Morgan Stanley, Starbucks, BNY, and PayPal used these scores to filter candidates before a human recruiter ever glanced at an application. The case argues that if Eightfold generates match scores based on these data, it is entitled to full rights that every candidate it scored was entitled to disclosure, and dispute. The complaint alleges that the behavior of third parties compiling profiles is illegal.
## Story
## Entities
### Companies
- PayPal Holdings, Inc.
- Crunchbase
- Starbucks Corporation
- Equifax Inc.
- Microsoft Corporation
- Morgan Stanley
- Workday, Inc.

## Description
Join us as we dive into the world of AI at LlamaCon 2025. This session features a keynote presentation with Chief Product Officer Chris Cox, VP of Llama Manohar Paluri, and Llama Researcher Angela Fan. Together, they will explore the importance of open-source collaboration and celebrate the achievements of the developer community. Discover the latest advances in Llama and get a glimpse of what's next in AI innovation.
Following the keynote, don't miss the fireside chat with Meta Founder and CEO Mark Zuckerberg and Databricks Co-founder and CEO Ali Ghodsi. They will discuss the critical role of open-source AI and share valuable advice for founders building AI-enabled applications.

## Description
"What is the Language Model of a Crow?"
Ali Farhadi is the CEO of the Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) and a Professor at the Paul G. Allen School of Computer Science & Engineering at the University of Washington. Ali has been a professor at the UW Allen School since 2012. He first joined Ai2 in 2015 to lead the institute’s computer vision research team
with a focus on visual common-sense reasoning and the role of actions and interactions in visual understanding. While at Ai2, he co-founded Xnor.ai, the first on-device deep learning startup that was acquired by Apple in 2020. Ali led Apple’s next-generation machine learning efforts until July 2023, when he
rejoined Ai2 as CEO. Ali’s research impact has been globally recognized with several best paper awards at CVPR, NeruIPS, AAAI, NSF Career Award, and the Sloan Fellowship, and he was named one of Forbes’s Top 5 AI Entrepreneurs in 2018.

# Building a Brutal Meeting Feedback Loop
Published: 2026-04-14T08:11:06.897000+00:00
## Summary
I use Granola to record and transcribe my meetings and use an MCP server to transfer my meeting notes and transcripts directly to Claude Code, who then creates a system that systematically evaluates communication across a set of defined dimensions. The system involves a detailed evaluation of communication across three categories, including word choice, Conviction, consistency, and accuracy. Each score comes with a detailed critique and a quote from the transcript as evidence. Each dimension is classified as red, yellow, or green, with each score determined by a "brutal question" that anchors the evaluation. Claude Code uses this system to ensure that the best communication is clear and clear. He also identifies key issues in communication, such as explaining technical decisions clearly enough for the PM and avoiding over-emphasizing technical details.
## Story
I leave meetings not knowing how I did. Not whether the meeting was productive — that part is obvious. Whether I communicated well. Did I explain the technical decision clearly enough for the PM to follow? Did I hedge when I should have committed to a position? Did I spend three minutes circling a point I could have made in thirty seconds? You do not get this feedback naturally. Nobody pulls you aside after a standup to say “you used the word ‘basically’ eleven times and your VP had no idea what you meant by ‘decouple the ingestion layer.’” The feedback that does come is vague: “that went well” or “I think people were confused.” Not useful. I wanted feedback that was specific, evidence-based, and harsh. Not encouraging, not balanced. The kind of critique that makes you wince because it quotes your own words back at you and explains exactly why they failed. I use Granola to record and transcribe my meetings. Granola has an MCP server, which means Claude Code can pull my meeting notes and transcripts directly. The idea was to build a Claude Code skill that fetches a meeting from Granola, reads through what I actually said, and evaluates my communication across a set of defined dimensions. The key design decision was evaluating every meeting from two perspectives simultaneously: a confused non-technical stakeholder and a ruthless domain expert. These are the two ways you can fail in a mixed meeting. You can lose the business people by drowning them in jargon. Or you can lose the engineers by hand-waving through technical details you do not actually understand. Most meetings have both audiences, so both lenses apply every time, at full intensity. I ended up with twelve dimensions across three categories. Each one has a “brutal question” that anchors the evaluation. Communication Craft — how you say it, regardless of audience: • Word Choice — “Did you use the right word, or the easy word?” • Conviction — “Did you sound like someone who knows, or someone who hopes they’re right?” • Narrative — “If someone tuned in mid-sentence, could they tell where you were going?” • Conciseness — “How many of your words could be deleted without losing meaning?” • Jargon Accessibility — “Would your VP of Sales know what you just said?” • Analogy Quality — “Did your analogy make them smarter, or just make them nod?” • So-What Clarity — “If they forgot everything else, would they still know what matters and why?” • Actionability — “Did you end with a clear ask, or just... stop talking?” • Technical Accuracy — “Would an expert in the room wince at anything you said?” • Depth vs. Hand-Waving — “If someone asked ‘why?’ one more time, would you have an answer?” • Rigor of Reasoning — “Could someone poke a hole in your argument in under 10 seconds?” • Nuance & Edge-Case Awareness — “Did you show you know where this breaks, or did you pretend it doesn’t?” Each dimension gets a red, yellow, or green score. Not a 1–5 scale. Red means you failed. Yellow means you got by but a stronger communicator would have done it better. Green means you nailed it. The instruction to the evaluator is to default to yellow and pick the harsher color when in doubt. Green has to be earned. The whole thing is a single markdown file at . No code, no dependencies. When I run , Claude fetches my most recent meeting from Granola via MCP. I can also pass a search term like to target a specific meeting. It pulls both the transcript and the summary, infers the meeting type and my role, and evaluates all twelve dimensions against what I actually said. Every score comes with a one-liner critique and a direct quote from the transcript as evidence. No vague feedback. Your own words, thrown back at you. After the scorecard, Claude asks if I want to drill into any dimension. I can ask why something was scored red, get advice on how to fix it, or have it re-read a specific section of the transcript. It stays in harsh critic mode throughout. No softening, no “but you did great overall.” Each scorecard gets saved to with YAML frontmatter tracking the date, meeting type, and score counts. Over time this builds a log I can review for patterns. Am I getting fewer reds on Jargon Accessibility? Is my Conviction score consistently yellow? The trend matters more than any single evaluation. I brainstormed this with Claude before building anything. The biggest decisions were about what to evaluate and how harsh to be. I started with three vague criteria: expressiveness, clarity, and whether what I said made sense to a layperson. Through a series of one-question-at-a-time exchanges, these expanded into twelve specific dimensions. Expressiveness alone split into three: word choice, conviction, and narrative structure. “Makes sense to a layperson” became four dimensions under the Layperson Lens. Adding the Expert Lens gave the evaluation its dual-perspective structure. The scoring system was originally 1–5. I changed it to red/yellow/green because a three-color system forces a clear verdict. You cannot hide in a 3. You are either failing, getting by, or nailing it. I also considered weighting dimensions based on who was in the meeting — going lighter on the Expert Lens for exec presentations, for example. I dropped this. Both lenses apply every time. No excuses based on audience. Communication skill is invisible to the person communicating. You cannot evaluate your own clarity in the moment because you already understand what you mean. You need an external perspective that reads your words with fresh eyes and tells you where they failed. Building that perspective into a tool you run after every meeting turns communication from a vague soft skill into something you can measure, track, and deliberately improve.

# The AI Paradox: Why Every LLM Call Is Funding Your Own Replacement | by Nilesh Kumar Singh | Apr, 2026 | Medium
Published: 2026-04-26T18:12:57.547000+00:00
Author: Nilesh Kumar Singh
## Summary
The author argues that the advantage AI creates is not exclusive, but it increases participant dependency and pricing power. Platform value increases as more participants join, and the more deeply integrated a platform into core workflows increases switching costs. This dependency can take years and significant engineering capital to unwind. The author compares this to Amazon's use of seller data to identify which third-party products gained traction and gave them preferential placement. AI capabilities are often embedded in systems that process customer data, generate content, support your decisions, and automate your operations. This trap traps companies who become dependent on a small number of providers who hold all the power, all the usage data, and all the roadmap leverage. No individual company can afford to defect from the platform, but the only winner is the model provider.

## Description
AI agents aren't just writing code anymore, they're building the infrastructure behind it. Databricks CEO Ali Ghodsi breaks down what he's seeing across 20k enterprise customers: vibe coding going mainstream, the collapse of traditional SaaS, and why his company just hit $5.4 billion in revenue growing 65% a year. This is what the AI economy actually looks like under the hood.

Thanks[Ali Ghodsi](https://www.linkedin.com/in/alighodsi/)for being so open, honest and developer-ish during our fireside chat last night (Poland time). I really enjoyed it very much and hope for more similar chats in the future.
I was nominated to the[Databricks Beacons](https://databricks.com/discover/beacons)program almost a year ago (which I consider similar to the other programs I’d been part of already: the[Confluent Community Catalyst](https://www.confluent.io/nominate/)and the[Java Champion](https://dev.java/community/jcs/)).
I must admit I haven’t yet learnt how to use these “venues” for better myself and the others around me. I’ve been doing my job as an IT freelancer and these recognitions just happened. So be it.
That’s when I really got to know[Karen Bajza-Terlouw](https://www.linkedin.com/in/karencbajza/)better who had been supporting my Databricks journey for quite some time already. She’s been supporting myself to find my own place in this world of Databricks the Platform where the commercial versions of ApacheSpark and DeltaLake (apparently) flourish.
There have always been[Jules S. Damji](https://www.linkedin.com/in/dmatrix/)and[Denny Lee](https://www.linkedin.com/in/dennyglee/)who have been so instrumental in my career as an Apache Spark and Delta Lake freelancer (but thought I’d focus on Karen more here and let them enjoy less important role in this “Ali’s event” story. Sorry mates).
So Karen called me a couple of months ago about this unique opportunity for Databricks Beacons to join a fireside chat with the one and only Ali Ghodsi.
To be really really honest, in my eyes Ali’s more of a businessman than a software engineer like[Matei Zaharia](https://www.linkedin.com/in/mateizaharia/),[Michael Armbrust](https://www.linkedin.com/in/michaelarmbrust/)or[Tathagata Das](https://www.linkedin.com/in/tathadas/)and the others from the[Apache Spark](https://spark.apache.org/)open source project’s crew who I’ve always been so excited to learn Spark or Delta Lake from, but given his role (the CEO) in Databricks I was quite interested to join the event (mind the word*“quite”*as I was not really convinced).
As it happens when I’m not really convinced I tentatively accepted the invitation (almost considering it a no-go call right when I clicked the Accept button).
Later, it turned out that the only time to have this chat was…cough…cough…1:00 AM Poland time! Imagine what I thought when I saw this weird time. Given it was supposed to happen on Thursday 1am and I was going to play basketball a couple of hours before that time I knew I’d be very tired and really not for such an apparently-boring-yet-ego-boosting event.
And yet Karen did a great job and convinced me to join the chat with Ali despite all these “unreal” obstacles.
And the day came and I joined the fireside chat with Ali.
Gee, that was so much fun actually!
There were[Adi Polak](https://www.linkedin.com/in/👋-adi-polak-68548365/)(2:00 AM Israel time),[Simon Whiteley](https://www.linkedin.com/in/simon-whiteley-uk/)(12:00 AM / a midnight UK time) and myself (1:00 AM Poland time) representing the other side of the pond with the people from the US: R. Tyler Croy, Denny, Karen and the main guest himself Ali.
Given the timezone differences and the sleepy and tired faces of us already after or at the midnight the chat kicked in with a warm welcome from Denny who asked us to introduce ourselves and share our thoughts on what we like and dislike about Delta Lake and Databricks’ style of handling things in the open source community.
Adi Polak shared her take first.
I’m not so sure Adi finished before Ali took the stage and shared his based on Adi’s (it’s only now when I noticed how close their names are not to mention that Adi’s surname is a Polish word for someone who lives in Poland!)
It was clear right off the bat that Ali is a fantastic and very engaging speaker who I’m sure would make a career in a social media space easily, e.g. as a YouTuber with 1MM after a couple of weeks :D
Ali addressed Adi’s points with such witty words and in such an engaging way that I immediately forgot it was 1AM and it’s gonna be 2AM when it all finishes.
Then Denny let me talk and then Simon followed by R. Tyler Croy. Every time we spoke it was clear it wouldn’t take long before Ali grabs the mic and starts talking. I didn’t mind actually. At some point this conversation was so open and friendly that no one really bothered to interrupt Ali anymore. That was a very eye-opening moment in the chat. We all really spoke what we felt.
At some point in this fireside chat it even looked like it was Ali’s interview when he claimed he’s doing this and that and “I show you this” was all over the place. He really wanted to impress us! And he did!
Ali shared how much he values open source and how much he wants Databricks to open source most if not all of the great features of Delta Lake. If what he claimed were going to be part of the open source Delta Lake that would make a huge impact on Delta Lake’s adoption (and would surely confirm the number 1 position against Apache Iceberg and Hudi).
Ali no longer spoke like a CEO but more like a developer who simply open sourced all what he’s developed behind the curtains. He once mentioned Red Hat that brought memories of JBoss and how every commercial entity wanted to ditch it until they eventually embraced it. I think that’s when I heard this quote for the first time when Marc Fleury referred to JBoss:
> First they ignore you,
> then they laugh at you,
> then they fight you,
> then you win.
Same with Linux that Ali did mention too (but these memories are more vague in my head as I no longer work with Linux since the kernel v2.0).
Ali shared some of his non-CEO views on internal things at Databricks (e.g. a devrel team or perhaps a couple per product) and the paper about Photon he’s been working on with Matei and Reynold to be published soon. Nice stuff!
I liked the most his firm and strong desire to have developers to work in the devrel team of Databricks considering them more liaisons between Databricks’ product development teams and the open source communities of Delta Lake and Apache Spark.
If I were asked to judge the future of Databricks Inc. in open source, I’d express only positive words. Similar attempts have been in the works already (e.g. Denny’s initiative to foster development of the[Flink connector for Delta Lake](https://github.com/delta-io/connectors/tree/master/flink-connector/src)with Trino’s in the pipeline).
Thank you Karen and Denny to spearhead this and similar initiatives to teach Databricks Inc. be a good open source citizen (even Microsoft has finally learnt that OSS is a good thing in a long run). Wish you all the best!

I discuss proven strategies for expanding your business, increasing your customer base, and enhancing operational efficiency. The episode offers valuable advice for business owners and entrepreneurs looking to grow their organizations sustainably, highlighting the need for patience, communication, and effective leadership.

# The AI Reality Check: Why the CEO of a $62B AI Company Says We’re All Becoming Supervisors
[
![Aaron Rose](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*sxSIbQU5T64YSqDSEts1ag.png)
](https://medium.com/@aaron.rose.tx?source=post_page---byline--f17ffef6f5af---------------------------------------)
[Aaron Rose](https://medium.com/@aaron.rose.tx?source=post_page---byline--f17ffef6f5af---------------------------------------)
Follow

·1 day ago
[
](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https://medium.com/_/vote/p/f17ffef6f5af&operation=register&redirect=https://medium.com/@aaron.rose.tx/the-ai-reality-check-why-the-ceo-of-a-62b-ai-company-says-were-all-becoming-supervisors-f17ffef6f5af&user=Aaron+Rose&userId=392ce918ae14&source=---header_actions--f17ffef6f5af---------------------clap_footer------------------)
--
[](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https://medium.com/_/bookmark/p/f17ffef6f5af&operation=register&redirect=https://medium.com/@aaron.rose.tx/the-ai-reality-check-why-the-ceo-of-a-62b-ai-company-says-were-all-becoming-supervisors-f17ffef6f5af&source=---header_actions--f17ffef6f5af---------------------bookmark_footer------------------)

*The CEO of Databricks says complete automation is harder than we think, and humans will supervise AI “teams of interns” for years*
The headlines paint a familiar picture: artificial intelligence will either usher in a utopian future of abundance or trigger mass unemployment as machines replace human workers. Between breathless predictions of 80% job displacement and equally optimistic promises of effortless automation, the AI conversation has become polarized between extremes.
Yet for all the theoretical debates happening in conference rooms and comment sections, there’s a notable gap between the rhetoric and what’s actually happening inside the companies deploying AI at scale. While pundits theorize about transformation, the people building and implementing these systems are encountering a more nuanced reality.
This disconnect isn’t academic —it has real implications for how businesses plan their AI strategies, how workers prepare for technological change, and how society adapts to an increasingly automated world. The difference between expectation and reality could shape everything from workforce development policies to corporate investment decisions.
But there’s one voice that cuts through both the hype and the fear with the authority that comes from actually building the systems everyone’s talking about.
# Meet Your AI Reality Check: Ali Ghodsi
Ali Ghodsi doesn’t just have opinions about artificial intelligence —he builds the infrastructure that powers it for some of the world’s largest companies. As co-founder and CEO of Databricks, a data analytics company valued at $62 billion, Ghodsi oversees the platform that more than 15,000 organizations worldwide use to implement AI solutions, including over 60% of the Fortune 500.
His technical credentials run deep. Ghodsi was one of the original creators of Apache Spark, the open-source framework that processes much of the world’s big data. Before building Databricks, he served as an adjunct professor at UC Berkeley, where his academic research in distributed systems laid the groundwork for modern data processing at scale.
This isn’t a Silicon Valley executive offering theoretical insights from the sidelines. Companies like Shell, Comcast, Block, and Rivian rely on Databricks’ platform for their AI implementations. When Ghodsi speaks about the challenges of automation, he’s drawing from direct experience with hundreds of enterprise deployments across industries.
Which makes his recent comments about AI automation particularly noteworthy. In a recent interview with Business Insider, speaking at a conference in San Francisco, Ghodsi offered a perspective that challenges both the apocalyptic predictions and the utopian promises currently dominating the conversation: “People underestimate how hard it is to completely automate a task.”
Coming from someone whose company has just unveiled a new platform for creating AI agents without code, this isn’t the message you might expect. But it reflects what Ghodsi sees happening across Databricks’ extensive customer base —a reality that’s more complex and nuanced than the simplified narratives suggest.
# The “Team of Interns” Metaphor: Reframing AI’s Real Role
Rather than the dramatic transformation scenarios that dominate headlines, Ghodsi envisions a more practical evolution: “I think in a few years, yes, we’ll have agents in many, many places, but there will be a human overseeing and approving every step, and you’re on the hook when you approve, when you click, ‘Okay.’ We all become supervisors.”
This supervision model fundamentally reframes how we think about AI in the workplace. Instead of artificial intelligence replacing human workers, Ghodsi describes a future where AI agents function more like a team of capable but mistake-prone interns that each employee manages.
Consider how this plays out in practice. At companies using Databricks’ platform, AI agents handle tasks like HR onboarding processes and answering questions about company policies. These aren’t trivial applications —they represent real productivity gains and meaningful automation. But they operate under constant human oversight, with employees reviewing and approving each action.
This model aligns with what we’re seeing in early AI implementations across industries. Take Klarna, which reported that its customer service AI agents could handle the workload of approximately 700 human employees. While impressive in scope, the implementation still requires human supervision for complex cases and maintains accountability structures that keep people in the decision-making loop.
Similarly, OpenAI’s Sam Altman has described current AI agents as functioning like “junior-level employees” —capable of handling routine tasks but requiring guidance and oversight for more complex decisions. This characterization supports Ghodsi’s supervision framework rather than the replacement narrative.
The implications of this shift are profound. If AI agents are more like interns than replacements, then the primary job transformation isn’t about displacement but about elevation. Employees move from executing routine tasks to managing AI systems that execute those tasks. The work changes, but it doesn’t disappear.
This supervision model also explains why AI adoption in enterprises has been more gradual than many predicted. Companies aren’t just implementing technology; they’re rebuilding workflows to include oversight mechanisms, training employees to manage AI systems effectively, and establishing accountability chains that ensure human responsibility for automated decisions.
# The Aviation Analogy: Why Humans Stay in the Loop
To illustrate why complete automation remains elusive, Ghodsi draws a compelling parallel to commercial aviation. “Why do we still want two pilots in there? And why do we want the pilots to be trained —we don’t want them to be undertrained or asleep in the cockpit or drunk,” he explains. “I think it’s just many, many orders of magnitude harder to get that last mile out.”
The aviation industry offers perhaps the most instructive example of sophisticated automation alongside persistent human oversight. Modern aircraft can take off, navigate, and land with minimal human input. Autopilot systems manage routine flight operations with remarkable precision. Yet decades after these technologies matured, commercial flights still require two trained pilots in the cockpit.
This isn’t due to regulatory inertia or resistance to change. It reflects a fundamental truth about complex systems: the final percentage points of reliability are exponentially more difficult to achieve than the initial automation. A system that works 95% of the time might seem impressive, but in high-stakes environments, the difference between 95% and 99.9% reliability can be measured in lives and billions of dollars.
The technical challenges mirror what Ghodsi observes in AI implementations. Research from Patronus AI, a startup focused on evaluating large language models, found that the more steps an AI agent takes, the higher its error rate becomes. Each additional decision point introduces potential failure modes that compound across complex processes.
This compounding effect explains why automating complete workflows remains challenging even as individual tasks become increasingly automated. A customer service interaction might involve dozens of micro-decisions: understanding context, accessing relevant information, applying company policies, determining appropriate responses, and escalating when necessary. While AI can handle many of these steps effectively, the probability of error-free execution across all steps simultaneously remains problematic.
The aviation analogy also illuminates why society demands human accountability even in highly automated systems. When autopilot systems function perfectly, passengers barely notice the pilots. But when unusual situations arise —severe weather, mechanical issues, or unexpected air traffic scenarios —human judgment becomes indispensable. The pilots aren’t just backup systems; they’re the accountable decision-makers who bear responsibility for passenger safety.
Similarly, in business contexts, AI agents can handle routine operations efficiently, but complex or exceptional situations require human judgment. More importantly, organizations need humans who can be held accountable for decisions made by artificial intelligence. As Ghodsi puts it: “Given that the AIs occasionally get things wrong, in society, we want somebody to be responsible.”
# The Technical Reality: Why Complete Automation Proves So Difficult
The gap between AI capabilities in controlled demonstrations and real-world deployment reflects fundamental technical challenges that aren’t easily solved by more computing power or larger datasets. Ghodsi’s perspective, informed by thousands of enterprise implementations, reveals why the “last mile” of automation remains so elusive.
The complexity problem manifests in multiple dimensions. First, real-world business processes involve countless edge cases that don’t appear in training data or testing scenarios. A seemingly straightforward task like processing customer requests becomes complex when you account for incomplete information, conflicting priorities, unclear communication, and exceptional circumstances that fall outside standard procedures.
Second, the reliability requirements for enterprise applications far exceed what’s acceptable in consumer contexts. A chatbot that provides incorrect information 5% of the time might be tolerable for casual queries, but that same error rate becomes unacceptable when processing financial transactions, medical recommendations, or legal compliance requirements.
Databricks’ experience across Fortune 500 implementations provides unique insight into these challenges. Companies in regulated industries face particular constraints —financial services firms can’t deploy AI systems that occasionally misinterpret compliance requirements, and healthcare organizations can’t accept systems that sometimes provide incorrect medical guidance.
The research backing Ghodsi’s observations extends beyond anecdotal experience. Patronus AI’s findings on agent error rates reveal a systematic pattern: as AI systems attempt more complex, multi-step processes, their reliability decreases predictably. This isn’t a temporary limitation that will disappear with the next generation of models —it reflects fundamental challenges in maintaining coherence and accuracy across extended reasoning chains.
Understanding these technical constraints helps explain why successful AI implementations focus on augmentation rather than replacement. Instead of attempting to automate entire job functions, effective deployments identify specific tasks where AI can provide reliable assistance while maintaining human oversight for complex decisions and exceptional cases.
This technical reality also shapes how companies should approach AI investments. Rather than expecting transformative automation that eliminates human involvement, organizations achieve better results by designing systems that enhance human capabilities while preserving accountability and oversight mechanisms.
The implications extend to workforce planning and development. If complete automation remains technically challenging for the foreseeable future, then the primary skill requirement shifts toward effective AI supervision and management rather than replacement anxiety or obsolescence fears.
# What This Means for the Future of Work
The supervision model that Ghodsi describes doesn’t represent a temporary waystation en route to full automation —it appears to be the sustainable paradigm for AI integration in knowledge work. This shift has profound implications for how we think about jobs, skills, and career development in an AI-enhanced economy.
The emergence of what we might call the “supervisor economy” suggests that most knowledge workers won’t see their jobs disappear but rather evolve. The core transformation involves moving from direct task execution to AI management and oversight. This evolution requires new skills: understanding AI capabilities and limitations, designing effective prompts and workflows, recognizing when AI output requires human intervention, and maintaining accountability for automated decisions.
Consider how this plays out across different functions. Marketing professionals might supervise AI systems that generate content drafts, analyze customer data, and optimize campaign performance, but retain responsibility for strategic decisions, brand consistency, and creative direction. Financial analysts might oversee AI models that process data and identify patterns, while focusing their human expertise on interpretation, risk assessment, and strategic recommendations.
This transformation also creates opportunities for productivity gains that benefit both employers and employees. When AI handles routine data processing, research, and administrative tasks, human workers can focus on higher-level analysis, relationship building, and strategic thinking. The result could be more engaging, valuable work rather than job displacement.
However, the supervision model requires intentional workforce development. Organizations need to invest in training employees to be effective AI supervisors rather than assuming the technology will be intuitive or self-managing. This includes both technical skills —understanding how AI systems work and how to interact with them effectively —and judgment skills for knowing when human intervention is necessary.
The long-term implications extend beyond individual companies to entire industries and educational systems. If Ghodsi’s assessment proves accurate, then workforce development programs should emphasize AI literacy and supervision skills rather than focusing primarily on technical implementation or replacement anxiety.
For business leaders, this perspective suggests a different approach to AI strategy. Instead of asking “Which jobs can we automate?” the more productive question becomes “How can we augment our employees’ capabilities while maintaining appropriate oversight and accountability?”
The supervision economy also implies that human skills like judgment, creativity, emotional intelligence, and complex problem-solving become more valuable, not less. These capabilities complement AI systems rather than competing with them, creating opportunities for career development that builds on uniquely human strengths.
# Practical Takeaways for Business Leaders
The insights from Ghodsi’s experience implementing AI across thousands of enterprise customers translate into actionable guidance for organizations developing their own AI strategies. Rather than chasing automation for its own sake, successful implementations focus on augmentation with robust oversight mechanisms.
First, organizations should design AI workflows that assume human supervision from the outset. This means building approval processes, exception handling, and accountability chains into systems rather than treating human oversight as a temporary measure. Companies that implement AI with the expectation of eventual full automation often create technical debt and operational risks that become expensive to address later.
Second, workforce development should emphasize supervision skills alongside technical capabilities. Employees need training not just in how to use AI tools, but in how to evaluate AI output, recognize edge cases that require human intervention, and maintain quality control over automated processes. This represents a different skill set than traditional technology training and requires dedicated development programs.
Third, success metrics should account for the supervision model rather than measuring pure automation rates. A system that automates 70% of routine tasks while maintaining high quality and appropriate human oversight may deliver better business value than a system that automates 90% of tasks but requires expensive error correction and creates liability risks.
Risk management also requires adaptation to the supervision paradigm. Organizations need clear policies about AI decision-making authority, accountability structures for automated actions, and escalation procedures for complex situations. The goal isn’t to eliminate human involvement but to ensure it’s effective and appropriately positioned.
Finally, AI strategy should align with business objectives rather than technology capabilities. The most valuable implementations focus on areas where AI can meaningfully enhance human productivity while preserving the judgment and accountability that complex business environments require.
These practical considerations reflect a broader shift in how organizations should think about artificial intelligence. Rather than viewing AI as a replacement technology, the supervision model positions it as an enhancement tool that amplifies human capabilities while preserving human responsibility and oversight.
# The Realistic AI Future
In a landscape dominated by polarized predictions about artificial intelligence, Ali Ghodsi’s perspective offers something increasingly rare: grounded insight from someone actually building and deploying these systems at enterprise scale. His assessment that “people underestimate how hard it is to completely automate a task” isn’t pessimism —it’s realism informed by thousands of real-world implementations.
The supervision model he describes represents neither the utopian vision of effortless automation nor the dystopian fear of mass displacement. Instead, it suggests a more nuanced future where AI agents function as sophisticated assistants requiring human oversight, where workers evolve into supervisors managing teams of artificial interns, and where the value of human judgment and accountability increases rather than diminishes.
This perspective has profound implications for how we prepare for an AI-enhanced future. Rather than anxiously awaiting replacement or passively expecting transformation, individuals and organizations can focus on developing the supervision and management skills that the technology actually requires. The future of work with AI appears to be more about evolution than revolution.
Perhaps most importantly, Ghodsi’s insights remind us to listen to the people actually building these systems rather than getting caught up in theoretical debates. The aviation industry learned decades ago that sophisticated automation enhances rather than replaces human expertise. The AI industry appears to be learning the same lesson.
In a world full of AI hype and speculation, the voice of practical experience offers a valuable anchor. The companies successfully implementing AI at scale aren’t eliminating human involvement —they’re reimagining it. And that might be the most realistic and sustainable path forward.
*Aaron Rose is a software engineer and technology writer.*

# Ali Ghodsi’s Missing MDS Class (Multidimensional Scaling)

![吴舒晨](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/0*Vc7DPx-RjhVreKDh.jpg)
](https://medium.com/@shuchen.wu2?source=post_page---byline--a27b20301bc---------------------------------------)
